preFix.title: Bakewell pudding
preFix.subtitle: The original Bakewell: flaky pastry with jam and a set almond and egg filling
preFix.excerpt: The Bakewell pudding is the original, older version of the better-known Bakewell tart. It has puff pastry rather than shortcrust, a layer of jam on the base, and a custard-like almond and egg filling that sets firm but stays slightly soft inside. It is still made in Bakewell, Derbyshire.
preFix.sourceNotes: English pudding originating in Bakewell, Derbyshire, dating from at least the early 19th century. The story that it was invented by accident at the White Horse Inn is widely cited but unverified. The pudding predates the Bakewell tart and uses puff pastry rather than shortcrust. It is still produced by specialist shops in Bakewell today.

content.text: The Bakewell pudding is not a tart. It has a puff pastry base and sides, a generous layer of jam, and a filling made from butter, sugar, eggs, and ground almonds that sets like a thick custard. It is richer than a sponge cake and should wobble very slightly in the centre when it comes out of the oven. It firms as it cools. Eat at room temperature, not hot.
Line the tin and make the filling

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Unroll the puff pastry and use it to line a shallow rectangular or oval baking tin, pressing it into the corners and trimming the edges to leave a 1 cm overhang. Spread the jam generously and evenly over the base.
Beat the softened butter and sugar together until pale. Gradually beat in the eggs a little at a time. Fold in the ground almonds and almond extract.

Spoon the almond filling over the jam and smooth it out to cover. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the top is golden brown. The centre should feel just set when pressed: it will wobble slightly if the tin is moved.
Leave to cool in the tin for at least 15 minutes before cutting. Serve at room temperature with double cream or custard.

items.fix: Place the tin on the bottom shelf of the oven for the first 15 minutes, then move to the middle shelf.
items.cause: Not enough heat reached the base.
items.symptom: The pastry base is pale and soft

items.fix: Make sure the butter is properly softened before starting, and add the beaten egg very gradually.
items.cause: Eggs added too quickly to cold butter.

items.fix: Use a good-quality thick jam. The filling should be thick enough to sit on top of the jam without mixing.
items.cause: Filling was too loose or jam was too thin.
items.symptom: The jam has bubbled up through the filling

content.text: The Bakewell pudding has been made in the Peak District town of Bakewell since at least the early 19th century. Two specialist bakers there still make it today, each claiming the original recipe. The more familiar Bakewell tart, with shortcrust pastry and a simpler almond sponge, is a later and separate dish. The pudding is richer and more custard-like, and is tied to Bakewell in the way that Eccles cakes are tied to Eccles.

initialFindings.message: historical century reference "19th century" in body — move to sourceNotes or introduce with a person (e.g. "the 17th-century herbalist X")
